title: Listening - Part 4: Organising a Community Festival
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Marcus Bell, the organiser of a community festival. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Marcus Bell, organiser of the Riverside Summer Festival, which attracts thousands of visitors each year. Marcus, how did you begin organising events? || Marcus: I wasn’t trained in events; I was a primary school teacher. During a summer when several local arts activities were cancelled, families complained that there was nothing affordable to attend. [24]I arranged a small afternoon of music and storytelling in the park simply because the neighbourhood seemed to need something cheerful[/24]. It was so well received that the council asked whether I would develop it. || Presenter: How do you choose performers now? || Marcus: At first I booked musicians whose work I enjoyed, which gave the festival a rather narrow character. [25]I now think about who will attend: children, teenagers and older residents all need to find at least one event that feels intended for them[/25]. That means I sometimes book acts I wouldn’t personally listen to at home. || Presenter: You rely heavily on volunteers. What have you learned about managing them? || Marcus: Volunteers are generous but may only work one day each year, so they cannot remember complicated systems. [26]Giving each person a clear, limited responsibility works much better than handing out a long list of general instructions[/26]. Some welcome visitors, others check recycling bins or guide performers. The festival feels calmer as a result, and volunteers are more likely to return the following summer. || Presenter: Is weather your greatest concern? || Marcus: It is one of them. We can move certain activities into the sports hall, but not all, and hiring covered equipment is expensive. [27]The hard decision is whether to activate the wet-weather plan early, when forecasts can still change and doing so costs money[/27]. Waiting too long can make the site unsafe. || Presenter: Do residents ever object to the festival? || Marcus: Most are supportive, but a festival affects their weekend. [28]People are far more patient about music and busy roads when we send them details beforehand and listen to concerns about parking or noise[/28]. Surprises cause more complaints than the event itself. || Presenter: Sponsorship must be important. || Marcus: It pays for free activities, so we value local businesses. Still, we are careful. [29]A sponsor can display its name, but it cannot decide which artists perform or turn a community event into a large advertisement[/29]. The public needs to trust the festival. || Presenter: How do you judge whether it has succeeded? || Marcus: Audience numbers matter, but they aren’t everything. [30]When visitors return the following year bringing neighbours or relatives, I feel we have created something people genuinely value[/30]. That is more meaningful than simply having one crowded afternoon.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Marcus arrange his first local event?

options.A: He was asked to do so as part of his teaching job.
options.B: He wanted to test a professional business idea.
options.C: He felt families lacked enjoyable local activities.

questions.explanation: He organised the first afternoon because the community needed something cheerful after activities were cancelled.

questions.question: How has Marcus changed the way he chooses performers?

options.A: He now considers the different groups in the audience.
options.B: He mainly chooses performers recommended by sponsors.
options.C: He gives preference to musicians from the local school.

questions.explanation: He books with children, teenagers and older residents in mind, not only his own taste.

questions.question: What has Marcus discovered about festival volunteers?

options.B: They work best when given specific tasks.
options.C: They need more training before the event.

questions.explanation: Clear, limited responsibilities are more effective than broad sets of instructions.

questions.question: What weather-related challenge does Marcus mention?

options.A: Finding enough buildings to use during rain.
options.B: Protecting musical equipment from strong sunshine.
options.C: Deciding when to commit to an expensive alternative plan.

questions.explanation: Activating the wet-weather plan early is costly while forecasts may still change.

questions.question: How does Marcus reduce complaints from residents?

options.A: He provides information in advance and considers their concerns.
options.B: He finishes all musical events before the afternoon.
options.C: He offers free tickets to everyone living nearby.

questions.explanation: Advance information and listening about traffic or noise make residents more patient.

questions.question: What does Marcus say about sponsors?

options.A: Their involvement should be limited to providing food.
options.B: They should not control the festival programme.
options.C: They prefer to support events with small audiences.

questions.explanation: He permits sponsors to show their name but not choose artists or dominate the event.

questions.question: What particularly shows Marcus that the festival is successful?

options.A: Visitors recommend it by bringing other people later.
options.B: It achieves a larger audience than nearby festivals.
options.C: The council agrees to offer additional funding.

questions.explanation: He values visitors returning in following years with relatives or neighbours.
